articles.title: Heat causes mobile phones to shut down in Rajasthan: Raises explosion risk; local batteries turning devices... - Bhaskar English
articles.full_text:
Extreme heat causes mobile phones to shut down in Rajasthan:Raises explosion risk; local batteries turning devices into time bombs
The scorching heat in Rajasthan is now affecting electronic devices as well. Recently, a grocery merchant in Jodhpur suffered burns to his thigh and fingers when a mobile phone kept in his pants pocket exploded like a bomb.
Following the incident, the Bhaskar team visited Raigar Plaza and Ganpati Plaza, Jaipur's tech hubs, where many shopkeepers reported a rise in complaints related to mobile overheating, battery swelling, sudden shutdowns, and even battery explosions.
According to tech experts, when temperatures soar between 45°C and 48°C, smartphones and their lithium-ion batteries come under increased stress, making devices more prone to overheating and related problems. Read the full report…
Customers arriving with complaints of battery swelling
Vishal Chaudhary, a resident of Sodala, who works as a bike rider for Uber, arrived at Ganpati Plaza in Jaipur with his mobile. For the past 3 days, his mobile was getting very hot. It had become difficult to talk on the phone.
The mobile would start getting hot after talking for a while or as soon as the internet was turned on. Upon showing it to the mechanic, he was advised to turn off location and internet on the mobile for some time. But the problem is that if he does that, his work will be affected.
Arvind Singh Rajpurohit, who runs a mobile repair center at Raisar Plaza in Jaipur, says,
Due to the excessive heat, people are coming in more frequently with concerns. The most common complaints are about mobile phones getting hot and batteries getting damaged. Upon checking, no major hardware fault is visible. Complaints about mobile batteries swelling have become common. In such a situation, we are advising customers that if the heat is high and you are outside, keep your internet and location off. Turn them on only when needed. This will prevent the mobile from overheating.
Mobile battery safe up to 35°C only
Tech expert and consultant in telecom companies, Mahesh Sethi, stated that the problem of phone heating is mostly related to the battery. Lithium-ion batteries generally perform well up to 35°C. As soon as the mobile phone's temperature exceeds 35 degrees Celsius, the system also reduces its responsiveness.
The chemical reaction between the lithium ions inside the battery accelerates. This causes the battery to swell. During charging, the radiation around the mobile also remains high. When heated, the mobile phone's internal motherboard can short-circuit and explode.
Mahesh Sethi explains,
Most of the incidents of mobile phones exploding that come to light involve mobile phones that are not from good companies. Sometimes, a local battery is used in a company's mobile phone. Due to repeated heating, that battery swells up.
Phones shut down automatically due to overheating
Mahesh Sethi explains that some mobile companies nowadays are providing an overheat alert function. As soon as the mobile's temperature goes above 35 degrees, the software automatically shuts down. This reduces the risk of the mobile exploding. However, this function is only available in some expensive models.
Those whose phones do not have such options should immediately stop using the mobile if it overheats. Shift the mobile to a low-temperature place as soon as possible. This can avert potential danger.
What is the correct way to charge a smartphone?
Mahesh Sethi advises users to take extra care of their phones during summer. Batteries should ideally be charged when they drop to around 10% and not beyond 85%. If the battery drains quickly or shows signs of damage, it should be replaced immediately with a quality battery from a reputed brand.
He also cautions against using phones while charging, especially for gaming, as it increases heat and battery stress. Keeping the internet off can help charge the phone faster and reduce battery strain. Users should also avoid overcharging, as it can shorten battery life and increase the risk of battery damage.
Do not keep mobile phones in closed car and bike trunks
In Jaipur, Jodhpur, Bikaner, Sriganganagar, Churu, Barmer, and Jaisalmer, temperatures are consistently being recorded above 45°C. Fearing their mobile phones might explode, some people keep them in the bike's storage compartment.
Some put them on the dashboard of a closed car. Inside a closed car, the temperature can reach 60 to 70°C, which is an extremely dangerous situation for any smartphone.
Negligence also a major cause
Navneet Pathak, president of the All India Mobile Retailer Association, stated,
Most mobile battery blast incidents are not due to a brand's quality, but rather due to negligence, fake batteries, non-certified chargers, unauthorised repairs, and excessive heat. Today's modern lithium-ion batteries are manufactured with high safety standards. Overheating can make them risky. Especially in extreme heat like Rajasthan, leaving the phone in direct sunlight, a car, or enclosed spaces can prove dangerous. Therefore, use only original chargers and certified accessories. If the phone is getting unusually hot, the battery is swelling, or any defect is visible, immediately get it checked at an authorized service center. Awareness, caution, and quality are the biggest guarantees of safety.
These mobile blast cases have come to light in Rajasthan
Case-1: Mobile explodes in pant pocket, merchant suffers severe burns
On June 8, 2026, a mobile phone kept in the pants pocket of grocery merchant Gopal Soni exploded in the Mandore Krishi Mandi area of Jodhpur. The phone caught fire, severely burning his thigh and fingers, with skin peeling off in some places.
Soni said he was sitting at his shop around 3:30 PM and checking a counter drawer when he heard a loud blast. His pants caught fire, and while removing the burning phone from his pocket, his fingers were also burnt.
Case-2: Charging mobile explodes, teenager seriously injured
On May 18, 2024, a mobile phone connected to charging exploded in the Kanwas area of Kota district. The blast seriously injured a 14-year-old boy, tearing the flesh on his palm and causing severe injuries to his fingers and hand.
Case-3: Man dies after mobile explosion
On February 25, 2024, Jagmal (44) of Malvas, Banswara, died after a mobile phone exploded while being charged. As soon as he plugged the charger into the electricity board, the mobile, charger and socket exploded.
The blast caused a severe chest injury, and Jagmal collapsed unconscious. He was rushed to hospital, where doctors declared him dead.
